message:
beads: open ft-pp7jk (disk_pressure threshold NaN guard)

Tracker bookkeeping for ft-pp7jk (PressureThresholds::normalized NaN
slip-through). Status in_progress.

Corresponding code fix landed in b0cce4da (fix(disk_pressure): NaN
thresholds fall back to Default in PressureThresholds::normalized).
Companion to ft-761tz which fixed the sample side.

Also re-emits the ft-f48yx entry (close_reason unchanged) — cosmetic
jsonl rewrite from the concurrent bd/br sweep; no semantic change.

No code changes in this commit.

Verification: rch cargo test blocked on remote CC=/opt/homebrew/... path missing on Linux worker; local cargo test reached unrelated existing compile errors in vendored/mux_client.rs and web_framework.rs after this slice compiled.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-eqtcq","title":"aegis_entropy_anomaly::EProcess::update propagates NaN e_value; permanently disables rejection","description":"EProcess::update calls likelihood_ratio(h, baseline_mean, baseline_std, collapse_mean, collapse_std). If any f64 input is NaN, log_h0 and log_h1 both become NaN, log_lr = NaN - NaN = NaN, log_lr.exp() = NaN, and clamp(1e-6, 1e6) passes NaN through because f64::clamp does not reject NaN. self.e_value *= NaN = NaN. The final clamp(1e-100, max_e) at update:226 also passes NaN through. Future is_rejected() returns (NaN >= threshold) = false, silently disabling anomaly detection for the lifetime of this EProcess. Matches ft-b4l62 / ft-yskcu / ft-c17iw / ft-n4fdx pattern — fail-closed on non-finite inputs at the update() boundary.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:02.334443Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:05:25.492018Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:05:25.491748Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}
 {"id":"ft-fq73h","title":"robot_types contract coverage omits current mission/tx error codes","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:14:29.145581Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T12:22:41.332099Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T12:22:41.331762Z","close_reason":"Landed in 8f5afe60","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
@@ -218,6 +218,7 @@
 {"id":"ft-phbu5","title":"[HIGH] full_pipeline_mux_checkpoint_topology still cannot verify pane-tree rollback","description":"Commit 30172171 fixes the vacuous baseline roundtrip, but the test still reduces "topology" to a pane-id set derived from LifecycleRegistry records rather than the restored topology payload.

Evidence:
- `crates/frankenterm-core/tests/integration_mux_checkpoint_topology.rs:392-423` now compares only `server.registry().snapshot()` pane IDs/count against the baseline and then re-parses `initial_topo_json` as a secondary serde check.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_topology.rs:38-89` defines topology as windows, tabs, `active_tab_index`, and recursive `pane_tree`, not just pane membership.
- `crates/frankenterm-core/src/durable_state.rs:159-167,288-314` shows HeadlessMuxServer checkpoints/rollback persist and rebuild only `LifecycleRegistry::snapshot()` entities; there is no topology JSON in the checkpoint path for the test to compare after rollback.

Why this matters:
- A rollback that preserves the same pane IDs but restores the wrong tab grouping, active tab, or split tree would still satisfy the current assertions.
- The test name and comments still claim topology restoration coverage, so this is false confidence on the checkpoint/rollback surface.

Expected fix:
- Either persist/restore topology payloads in the checkpoint path and compare a post-rollback `TopologySnapshot` against the baseline,
- or explicitly narrow/rename this test to registry-membership rollback and add a separate topology persistence/restore integration test.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T06:58:05.819749Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:23:00.021289Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:23:00.020891Z","close_reason":"Strengthened full_pipeline_mux_checkpoint_topology with structural pane-tree rollback invariant","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-phz7x","title":"codec: buffered_frame_len accepts oversized tagged_len, allowing memory-exhaustion before MAX_PDU_SIZE fires","description":"frankenterm/codec/src/lib.rs:240 buffered_frame_len validates only that the leb128-decoded payload_len fits in usize. It does NOT check against MAX_PDU_SIZE (256 MiB at lib.rs:106). An attacker who sends a frame whose outer tagged_len advertises e.g. 10 GiB forces try_read_and_decode at lib.rs:763-792 into a read loop that keeps extending the caller's buffer 4 KiB at a time until buffer.len() >= total_len — because stream_decode's first step is if buffered_frame_len(buffer)?.is_none() { return Ok(None); }, which short-circuits to 'need more bytes' and never rejects oversize. decode_raw at lib.rs:395-403 DOES reject via MAX_PDU_SIZE, but by then the process has already OOM'd or swapped. Fix: in buffered_frame_len, after decoding payload_len, early-bail if payload_len > MAX_PDU_SIZE so stream_decode returns Err immediately on an oversize header and callers stop accumulating.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:22:57.249807Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:53.618256Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:27:53.617831Z","close_reason":"Landed in 22965865 (fix(codec): reject oversize tagged_len before accumulation in buffered_frame_len). Concurrent-agent commit landed the identical fix + regression test I wrote: MAX_PDU_SIZE early-reject in buffered_frame_len before try_read_and_decode accumulates attacker-advertised gigabytes. Test buffered_frame_len_rejects_oversize_tagged_len_ft_phz7x passes in the 117-test codec suite.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-pmpxj","title":"vendored mux_client pane-delta helper regression blocks frankenterm-core build","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:59:50.368556Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:09:43.522568Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:09:43.522022Z","close_reason":"Verified in bd9b2d99; narrow vendored rebuild no longer reports pane_delta helper errors before hitting later blockers","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"ft-pp7jk","title":"policy RateLimiter pane_counts leak still present: ft-yjt9e methods unwired in PaneDestroyed handler","description":"Follow-up to ft-yjt9e (closed in 27ef45e1 + f97e6cac): the tools were added (RateLimiter::remove_pane, gc, gc_at, tracked_pane_entry_count and PolicyEngine::remove_pane/gc_rate_limiter forwarders) but nothing in the runtime teardown path actually calls them. Confirmed by reading runtime.rs:3406 (NativeEvent::PaneDestroyed handler): it tears down cursors, detection_contexts, pane_activity_tracker, and backpressure.cleanup_pane but never touches PolicyEngine. Consequence: the original memory leak ft-yjt9e described (every (pane_id, ActionKind) pair in RateLimiter.pane_counts lingers forever after pane destroy, estimated ~48 MB per 10k destroyed panes in long-running watchers) is still present in production. Two wiring options: (a) thread an Arc<Mutex<PolicyEngine>> through to the runtime so PaneDestroyed can call engine.remove_pane(pane_id) directly; (b) fan out a PaneDestroyed event via the event bus and have the workflow/policy layer subscribe. Option (b) matches the existing fanout architecture and avoids coupling runtime to the policy layer, but requires adding a subscriber. Option (a) is surgical and runtime-local. Recommend (b) for layering. Test: after wiring, a 1000-pane-churn loop must show tracked_pane_entry_count() stays bounded, not linear in total-panes-ever-destroyed.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:26:47.948045Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:26:47.948045Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-ptjlp","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses OutboundEvent.timestamp_ms as clock — same replay-bypass shape as ft-dijpe (inbound)","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027` in `process_event`:

```rust
if event.correlation_id.is_some()
    && !self
        .deduplicator
        .check_and_record(&correlation_id, event.timestamp_ms)
{ ... }
```

And line 1080:

```rust
let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms;
```

Both use `event.timestamp_ms` as the dedup/processing clock. `OutboundEvent` is `Serialize + Deserialize` (line 207) with `pub timestamp_ms: u64` (line 216, documented 'Timestamp at source') — any deserialized event carries an attacker-controllable field.

## Shape

Identical to ft-dijpe (connector_inbound_bridge, closed in 0716e112). An OutboundEvent with:

1. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=0 → inserted_at=0 in OutboundDeduplicator.
2. correlation_id='spam', timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1 → `evict_expired(now=ttl+1)` pops the live entry, `check_and_record` finds cache empty, inserts + returns `true` (new). Replay accepted.

Repeat indefinitely by monotonically increasing the claimed timestamp. Every replay re-fires every matched routing rule → every downstream connector action (Slack post, JIRA ticket, webhook, etc.) fires again without bound.

## Severity

MEDIUM — same as inbound. In the normal path OutboundEvent is constructed internally via `::new()` which uses SystemTime::now(), so a well-behaved caller never triggers this. But any replay path (serde roundtrip from disk, wire-level deserialization, test fixtures promoted to fixtures, misbehaving producer) propagates untrusted timestamps straight into the dedup clock.

## Fix

Mirror ft-dijpe: replace `event.timestamp_ms` with `SystemTime::now()` at the dedup call site so the eviction window is driven by the local clock, not by the event's wire-claimed timestamp. Leave `let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms` at line 1080 alone if it's only used for non-security accounting (history, telemetry); change only the dedup-clock input.

## Test

`outbound_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_event_timestamp` mirror of `bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_signal_timestamp` (added in 0716e112 for inbound):

* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=0 → not deduplicated.
* Route event A with cid='replay', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 → must be flagged deduplicated=true.

Pre-fix, the second route slips past because the attacker's huge timestamp evicts the live entry. Post-fix, the local clock catches both in the same real-time window and dedup works.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:24:49.120311Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:30.366903Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:29:30.366641Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 85a0f350. Mirror of ft-dijpe (inbound, 0716e112): dedup_now_ms now comes from SystemTime::now() instead of the Serialize+Deserialize OutboundEvent.timestamp_ms wire field. Regression test connector_outbound_bridge_dedup_ignores_attacker_controlled_event_timestamp (timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2 on replay → still flagged deduplicated=true) covers the same attacker-replay shape. Verified: 53 connector_outbound_bridge:: tests passed, 0 failed.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-q3gaz","title":"backpressure small-capacity queues fail closed to Black via saturating_sub saturation rule","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane8","created_at":"2026-04-22T10:42:07.199894Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.908513Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T10:44:19.907944Z","close_reason":"Tightened Black-tier saturation escalation so tiny capture/write queues no longer trip emergency mode via saturating_sub; added regressions for small-capacity empty/high-fill behavior.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"ft-qf3vk","title":"[HIGH] ft-ybtyg incomplete: session_checkpoints.state_hash is written but never verified on load","description":"Follow-up from ft-ybtyg (closed in 598a7c13).

## Gap

The ft-ybtyg fix replaced the placeholder `'restore'` literal with a real SipHash via `compute_restore_state_hash` and documented the intent: 'a later integrity check can recompute and compare'. But grep across session_restore.rs + snapshot_engine.rs + storage.rs finds ZERO callers that read the stored state_hash, recompute from the current inputs, and assert equality. The hash is written on INSERT, never verified on SELECT/load.

Result: the on-disk state_hash column now contains useful-looking SipHash values but is still effectively unverified — an attacker who tampers with the session_checkpoints row (flipping hash + any other field) is not detected, because no read path compares the stored hash against the recomputed hash.

## Reproducer
```
grep -rn 'compute_restore_state_hash\|compute_state_hash' crates/frankenterm-core/src/ | grep -iE 'verify|check|compare|assert_eq'
```
Expected matches: any call site that does `recomputed == stored`.
Actual: (empty).

## Fix direction
1. In the session-load path (session_restore.rs load fn + snapshot_engine.rs open/restore), after reading a session_checkpoints row, call compute_restore_state_hash on the current inputs and compare with the stored state_hash column.
2. On mismatch, return a typed Error (new StateHashMismatch variant) and refuse to restore from a tampered checkpoint.
3. Add regression test with a deliberately-tampered row that must produce StateHashMismatch and NOT a silent restore.

## Severity
MEDIUM → HIGH. The fix closed the docs-drift half but left the verification half open. A partial integrity check is worse than no check because it signals a stronger trust story than actually exists.

## Priority
P1 — this closes a real hole that ft-ybtyg was supposed to close.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T14:03:26.056799Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.029163Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T14:28:42.028931Z","close_reason":"fixed in ca3d3fe2 — state_hash verification wired into load_checkpoint_by_id for startup rows; StateHashMismatch error + 5 regression tests (tampered hash, tampered pane_id_map, legacy 'restore' literal, null metadata, non-startup skip). Periodic/capture checkpoints remain a documented follow-up since their hash is over PaneInfo not reconstructable from session_checkpoints alone.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
